title: Spreadsheet cell editing with formula dependency graph and live recalc
business_context: Google Sheets-clone for finance teams. 100k-cell sheets, 20 concurrent editors. Edit to A1 must recalc all dependents (B1=A1+5, C1=B1*2) and push deltas to all viewers under 300ms p95. Scale: 5k active sheets, 50 hot sheets with full editor load. If wrong: stale numbers in financial models = wrong decisions, audit trail gaps.
constraints: Recalc only touches affected cells, not full sheet
Edit conflict on same cell handled deterministically
Recalc must be transactional — viewers see consistent snapshot, never partial recalc
failure_modes_to_handle: Two users edit same cell with different formulas
Reconnect during recalc — must catch up to consistent state
architecture_reasoning:
1. Cells in Postgres with formula + computed value + version. Dependency edges in adjacency table (dependent_cell → depends_on_cell). On formula change: parse → diff old/new deps → update adjacency → BFS from changed cell over reverse adjacency → recalc each in topo order → write all in single tx → emit cellset diff.
2. Postgres chosen: recursive CTE for topo walk (WITH RECURSIVE), advisory lock per sheet for serialization, ACID for atomic 'all dependents updated' guarantee.
3. Bottleneck: large cascade (10k dependents). Mitigate with per-sheet advisory lock serializing recalcs; client UI shows 'calculating' badge for >100ms cascades. Server caches parsed formulas in Redis per (sheetId, cellId) keyed by formula hash to skip re-parse.
4. Adapter: Redis. sheet:{id} room. Fan-out is the diff (only changed cells), not full sheet.
5. Recovery: client tracks sheetVersion; on reconnect, GET /sheets/{id}/changes?since=version returns deltas.
6. Rejected: in-process recalc engine (no persistence = crash = stale numbers). Rejected client-side recalc (untrusted = audit nightmare).

database_design.indexes: cells PK is the access path. cell_deps reverse index (sheet_id, depends_on_ref) for cascade lookup. history append-only.
database_design.queries: Cascade BFS: WITH RECURSIVE rev(ref, depth) AS (SELECT depends_on_ref, 0 FROM cell_deps WHERE sheet_id=$1 AND depends_on_ref=$2 UNION SELECT cd.dependent_ref, r.depth+1 FROM rev r JOIN cell_deps cd ON cd.sheet_id=$1 AND cd.depends_on_ref=r.ref WHERE r.depth < 50) SELECT DISTINCT ref FROM rev. Update batch: UPDATE cells ... in topo order. Circular detection: if BFS depth exceeds 50 OR if changed_ref appears in its own descendants → error.
database_design.transaction_strategy: SELECT pg_advisory_xact_lock(sheet_id) → parse new formula → DELETE old cell_deps for ref → INSERT new ones → BFS dependents → topo sort → UPDATE each dependent cell with new value → UPDATE sheets.version = version + 1 → COMMIT. Single tx ensures atomicity. On circular detection mid-tx: ROLLBACK, return error to client.

socket_io_design.events: type CellDiff = { ref:string; value:any; err?:string; cellVersion:number }; Events: 'sheet:open' (C→S ack with cells + sheetVersion), 'sheet:sync' (C→S since), 'cell:edit' (C→S {ref, formula, expectedSheetVersion}), 'cells:changed' (S→C array of diffs + new sheetVersion), 'cell:error' (S→C, e.g. circular).
socket_io_design.adapter_choice: redis. Per-sheet affinity via consistent hash to minimize cross-node lock contention.

race_conditions_addressed: Two users edit same cell: pg_advisory_xact_lock serializes; second sees version mismatch (expectedSheetVersion stale) and gets refreshed snapshot.
Circular dep created mid-edit: BFS detection inside tx → ROLLBACK → user sees 'circular reference' error before any state changes.
Recalc cascade interrupted by crash: entire tx rolls back; cells remain at pre-edit versions (atomicity).
Reconnect during recalc: cell_version per cell + sheetVersion enables idempotent catch-up via sheet:open. Diffs already broadcast include cellVersion so duplicate-broadcast on retry is no-op.
scaling_considerations: 10x (50k sheets, 500 hot): per-sheet advisory lock fine — different sheets recalc concurrently. Hot sheet with 10k-cell cascade: split eval across worker pool (per-batch). 100x: move recalc engine to Rust worker via gRPC; PG only stores state.
observability: Metrics: recalc_cascade_size histogram, recalc_duration_ms p50/p99, circular_detect_count, edit_conflict_rate. Logs: every edit with userId, ref, formula. Traces: tx → parse → BFS → topo eval. Alerts: p99 recalc > 1s (hot sheet abuse), circular spike (formula UX bug) Runbook: inspect sock-026 by namespace, tenant, adapter node, room cardinality, and sequence gap; page the owner when trace spans show persistence succeeded but client ack did not arrive within 2s for two consecutive windows.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A naive solution for Spreadsheet cell editing with formula dependency graph and live recalc handles the demo path but misses the collaborative editing boundary: authorization, persistence, reconnect or retry behavior, rollback, and idempotency replay count are not proved under production-shaped fixtures.

title: E2E-encrypted DMs with double-ratchet key sync via socket
business_context: Privacy-first messenger (Signal-clone for enterprise). Messages are E2E encrypted client-side; server is blind to plaintext. Server's job: durable storage of ciphertext + reliable key bundle distribution. Scale: 1M users, 200 msgs/sec average. If server can read messages = product dead.
constraints: Prekey bundle rotation pushed to peers
Multi-device: each device has own key, messages fan out to all
Forward secrecy: old keys deleted after use
failure_modes_to_handle: Prekey exhaustion (sender can't start new session)
Device removed but still receives old ciphertext
Server compromise must not yield plaintext
architecture_reasoning:
1. Conversations in Mongo (durable ciphertext storage). PreKey bundles in Mongo collection per (userId, deviceId), one-time keys consumed on read with $pop atomicity.
2. Send: client generates ciphertext per recipient device → POST ciphertext bundle → server distributes to each device. Server validates recipient deviceIds belong to claimed userIds.
3. Real-time: socket emits 'msg:new' with ciphertext blob; client decrypts.
4. Key rotation: client pushes new prekey bundle via 'keys:replenish' when count < threshold. Server stores and serves on session establishment.
5. Mongo chosen: per-user device list as embedded array, change-stream could power presence (typing) without leaking content.
6. Adapter: Redis. user:{id}:device:{did} rooms (multi-device fan-out).
7. Forward secrecy: $pop on one-time key array atomically consumes — no double-use possible.

database_design.indexes: PreKeyBundle (userId) for device enumeration. EncMessage (recipientId, recipientDeviceId, deliveredAt) for undelivered fetch. TTL on expiresAt for disappearing messages.
database_design.queries: Consume one-time prekey: db.preKeyBundles.findOneAndUpdate({_id, 'oneTimePreKeys.0': {$exists:true}}, {$pop: {oneTimePreKeys: -1}}, {projection: {oneTimePreKeys: {$slice:1}}}). Undelivered fetch: db.encMessages.find({recipientId, recipientDeviceId, deliveredAt:null}).sort({serverTs:1}).limit(100). Delete on delivery: updateOne({_id},{$set:{deliveredAt:now}}).
database_design.transaction_strategy: Send bundle (one ciphertext per recipient device) in tx: insertMany ciphertext docs atomic so partial-fanout doesn't happen. Prekey consumption is single-op atomic via findOneAndUpdate.

socket_io_design.events: type EncMsg = { conversationId:string; senderId:string; senderDeviceId:string; ciphertext:string; messageType:number; serverTs:number }; Events: 'subscribe' (C→S {userId, deviceId}, ack with undelivered), 'msg:new' (S→C), 'msg:ack' (C→S {ids[]}), 'keys:replenish' (C→S new prekey bundle), 'keys:fetch' (C→S target userId → bundle).
socket_io_design.middleware_order: jwt-auth → device-binding-check (deviceId belongs to userId) → rate-limit

race_conditions_addressed: Concurrent prekey consumption: $pop is atomic in Mongo — two consumers can't get same one-time key.
Message delivered to removed device: server-side device check on subscribe rejects; old undelivered messages just expire via TTL.
Replay (server resends old ciphertext): Signal Protocol ratchet rejects replayed ciphertext at decrypt layer (client-side defense); server cannot forge new ciphertext anyway since it has no keys.
Multi-device send race: insertMany ordered:false ensures per-device delivery proceeds even if one fails; partial failure logged but most recipients still get message.
scaling_considerations: 10x (10M users, 2k msg/sec): EncMessage write rate fine in Mongo; sharding by recipientId hash if needed. Prekey replenishment is bursty — debounce client-side to replenish in batches of 100.
observability: Metrics: encMessage_insert_to_emit_ms, undelivered_queue_size, prekey_exhaustion_rate per device. Logs: NEVER log ciphertext or any decrypt attempt — only metadata (senderId, recipientId, ts). Alerts: prekey_exhaustion > 1% (clients not replenishing), undelivered_queue stale > 24h (devices abandoned). Traces: handler validation -> persistence/outbox -> Socket.IO emit -> client ack for sock-027, with tenant/user/room attributes and adapter-lag timing.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A naive solution for E2E-encrypted DMs with double-ratchet key sync via socket handles the demo path but misses the live chat boundary: authorization, persistence, reconnect or retry behavior, rollback, and query p95 latency are not proved under production-shaped fixtures.

title: SLA tracking dashboard with rolling window aggregation and burn-rate alerts
business_context: SRE team monitors service SLA (99.9% over 30d). Burn rate = current error budget consumption velocity. If burn rate exceeds 14.4x for 1h, page on-call (will exhaust budget in 30d). Scale: 200 services tracked, 50 viewers. If broken: SLO breaches detected too late = customer credit lost.
constraints: Page within 60s of burn-rate threshold crossing
Backfill data correction propagates to visible dashboards
failure_modes_to_handle: Burn rate hits threshold briefly then recovers (flap)
architecture_reasoning:
1. Events into Postgres time-series. Continuous aggregates roll up to 1min buckets.
2. Burn-rate computed by view: error_count/(allowed_error_budget) over 5min window. Triggered query every 30s for each (service, SLO) tracked.
3. Alert dispatch: if burn > threshold for 2 consecutive checks (anti-flap), open incident → page via PagerDuty.
4. Postgres chosen: SQL window functions perfect for burn-rate; LISTEN/NOTIFY for live dashboard.
5. Adapter: Redis. dash:sli:{svc} rooms.

database_design.indexes: sli_events hypertable + (service, ts DESC). slos PK. alerts (service, closed_at).
database_design.queries: Burn rate: WITH win AS (SELECT sum(errors) AS e, sum(total) AS t FROM sli_1m WHERE service=$1 AND bucket > now()-interval '5 minutes') SELECT CASE WHEN t=0 THEN 0 ELSE (e::numeric/t) / (1 - $target) END AS burn_rate FROM win. Live tail: query every 30s + LISTEN sli_burn channel.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Backfill correction: tx wrapping bulk INSERT into sli_events; continuous aggregate refreshes lazily within end_offset window.

socket_io_design.rooms: sli:{service}, incident:{incidentId} for room of viewers.

race_conditions_addressed: Flap: 2-consecutive-window streak counter (Redis INCR) requires sustained breach before paging.
Backfill correction: continuous aggregate refresh policy with end_offset=1m guarantees burn-rate query reads up-to-1min-stale data — backfill > 1min old visible at next tick.
Alert pageOnCall failure: try/catch fallback to email; alert row already inserted so resolution loop still works.
Multiple concurrent burn-rate checks for same SLO: 'existing alert open' check prevents duplicate alerts.
scaling_considerations: 10x (2000 services): sequential SLO loop too slow — parallelize with worker pool. 100x: switch to streaming aggregation (Materialize, Flink) instead of polling.
observability: Metrics: burn_rate_calc_duration_ms, alert_open_count, page_dispatch_success_rate, flap_suppressed_count. Logs: every alert open/close with burn rate. Alerts: page_dispatch failure > 0 (pager subsystem broken), burn_rate_calc lag > 60s. Logs: structured subscribe/emit/ack records with socketId, tenant/user id, room, sequence, permission decision, and reconnect token result. Traces: span from handler validation through persistence/outbox to Socket.IO emit and client ack. Alerts: ack_error_rate > 1%, reconnect_gap_count > 0.5% of sessions, fanout p99 > 500ms, or unauthorized_room_attempt_count > 0. Dashboard panels break down sock-028 by namespace, node, room size, and adapter lag.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A naive solution for SLA tracking dashboard with rolling window aggregation and burn-rate alerts handles the demo path but misses the real time dashboards boundary: authorization, persistence, reconnect or retry behavior, rollback, and state drift incidents are not proved under production-shaped fixtures.

title: Turn-based card game with action validation and spectator broadcast
business_context: Online card game (Hearthstone-like). 2 players per match + up to 1000 spectators. Server validates every move (anti-cheat). Spectators see delayed view (3s lag) to prevent stream-sniping. Scale: 5k concurrent matches. If wrong: cheaters dominate ladder → game dies.
constraints: Server is sole authority for game state
Move validation: turn order, mana cost, target legality
Game saved every action for replay
failure_modes_to_handle: Player submits action out of turn
Race: both players act simultaneously after timer
Spectator floods join causing fan-out lag
architecture_reasoning:
1. Match state lives in Postgres game_states table (JSONB). Action log in actions table. Each action: load state → validate → apply → persist new state + action → broadcast.
2. Per-match advisory lock serializes actions. Turn-based = low contention.
3. Spectator delay: separate delivery queue with 3s delay before fan-out. Actual players see immediately.
4. Postgres for ACID state updates + action log.
5. Adapter: Redis. match:{id}:players + match:{id}:spectators.

database_design.schema: CREATE TABLE matches (id BIGSERIAL PK, p1 BIGINT, p2 BIGINT, current_turn TEXT, state JSONB, version INT DEFAULT 0, status TEXT DEFAULT 'active', started_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now(), ended_at TIMESTAMPTZ);\nCREATE TABLE actions (id BIGSERIAL PK, match_id BIGINT REFERENCES matches(id), player_id BIGINT, action JSONB, applied_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now(), version_before INT, version_after INT);\nCREATE INDEX ON actions (match_id, id);
database_design.indexes: matches PK + (status). actions (match_id, id) for replay.
database_design.queries: Apply action: SELECT FOR UPDATE state, version → validate → UPDATE state, version → INSERT action.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Per-action tx with pg_advisory_xact_lock(match_id). Validation runs inside lock so no race.

socket_io_design.events: Events: 'match:join' (C→S {matchId} ack with role + state), 'action' (C→S {matchId, action, version}), 'state:update' (S→C immediate to players, delayed to spectators), 'turn:timer' (S→C countdown).

race_conditions_addressed: Both players act after timer expires: advisory lock serializes; second sees current_turn switched and gets 'not_your_turn'.
Stale version on retry: server returns current state + version; client refreshes UI; player can resubmit.
Spectator delay enforced server-side: clients cannot bypass since they receive update later regardless of client tampering.
Server restart: match state is in PG; on restart, sockets reconnect via match:join and resume from current state + version.
scaling_considerations: 10x (50k matches): per-match advisory lock isolates; total throughput limited only by PG write IOPS. 100x: shard matches table by id ranges; replicate read-only state for spectator queries.
observability: Metrics: action_validate_ms, action_apply_ms, invalid_action_count by reason, spectator_count gauge, replay_size_bytes. Logs: every action with playerId, validation result. Alerts: invalid_action_rate > 5% (cheat tool detected), action_apply p99 > 500ms. Logs: structured subscribe/emit/ack records with socketId, tenant/user id, room, sequence, permission decision, and reconnect token result. Traces: span from handler validation through persistence/outbox to Socket.IO emit and client ack. Alerts: ack_error_rate > 1%, reconnect_gap_count > 0.5% of sessions, fanout p99 > 500ms, or unauthorized_room_attempt_count > 0. Dashboard panels break down sock-029 by namespace, node, room size, and adapter lag.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A naive solution for Turn-based card game with action validation and spectator broadcast handles the demo path but misses the multiplayer game state boundary: authorization, persistence, reconnect or retry behavior, rollback, and cache stale hit rate are not proved under production-shaped fixtures.

title: NFT marketplace with bid notifications and ownership transfer on settlement
business_context: NFT marketplace: live auction per token, multiple parallel auctions. Highest bid + reserve met triggers transfer at end. Followers of seller get bid notifications. Scale: 1k concurrent auctions, 200 bidders each. If wrong: settlement bugs lose NFTs ($$$).
constraints: Auction end triggers atomic ownership transfer
failure_modes_to_handle: Watcher count grows beyond fan-out capacity
architecture_reasoning:
1. Auction state in Mongo. Bids appended to bid log; auction.currentBid materialized for O(1) read.
2. Settlement: end-of-auction worker locks doc, calls chain to transfer; on failure, retry with exponential backoff up to N times, then escalate to ops.
3. Adapter: Redis. auction:{id} room.
4. Watcher notifications use sock-008 pipeline.

database_design.indexes: Auction by status for scan. Bid by auction + nonce unique.
database_design.queries: Bid attempt: findOneAndUpdate auction with conditional $set current if amount > current. Replay protection via unique nonce.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Mongo session tx: load auction (FOR UPDATE-equivalent via findOneAndUpdate) → validate amount > current → INSERT bid → UPDATE auction.currentBid. Settlement tx: status='settling' lock then chain call → on success status='settled'.

socket_io_design.events: Events: 'auction:watch', 'bid' (HTTP for durability, socket for broadcast), 'bid:update' (S→C), 'auction:settled' (S→C), 'auction:failed' (S→C).

race_conditions_addressed: Two bids same block: Mongo $expr conditional update picks first; second gets 'too_low_or_closed' even if amount equal.
Settlement retry race: status='settling' lock; only one worker enters chain call.
End-of-auction mid-bid: $expr endsAt > now check rejects.
Unique (auction, bidder, nonce) prevents replay.
scaling_considerations: 10x (10k auctions): settlement loop scan needs index on (status, endsAt). 100x: per-shard settlement workers.
observability: Metrics: bid_throughput, settlement_success_rate, settlement_retry_count, watcher_count. Logs: every settlement attempt + tx hash. Alerts: settlement_failure_rate > 5%. Logs: structured subscribe/emit/ack records with socketId, tenant/user id, room, sequence, permission decision, and reconnect token result. Traces: span from handler validation through persistence/outbox to Socket.IO emit and client ack. Alerts: ack_error_rate > 1%, reconnect_gap_count > 0.5% of sessions, fanout p99 > 500ms, or unauthorized_room_attempt_count > 0. Dashboard panels break down sock-030 by namespace, node, room size, and adapter lag.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A naive solution for NFT marketplace with bid notifications and ownership transfer on settlement handles the demo path but misses the live auction boundary: authorization, persistence, reconnect or retry behavior, rollback, and cache stale hit rate are not proved under production-shaped fixtures.

title: Geo-presence with H3 spatial index for proximity-based fan-out
business_context: Field-ops app: drivers, technicians, supervisors see colleagues within 5km radius. Privacy: only same-org members. Scale: 50k field workers nationwide, 20Hz position updates. If wrong: massive fan-out (everyone seeing everyone) burns mobile data.
constraints: Proximity query: nearest N in 5km within 100ms
Fan-out only to viewers within proximity
failure_modes_to_handle: GPS jitter causing flap in proximity
Device offline reporting (cached coords) shouldn't trigger fan-out
architecture_reasoning:
1. Use H3 hierarchical spatial index (Uber's library). Each position → H3 cell at appropriate resolution. Proximity = adjacent cells.
2. Redis ZSET per H3 cell with userId → score=lastUpdate. SADD to org:{orgId}:cell:{h3}. On position update, remove from old cell, add to new.
3. Fan-out: viewer subscribes to current cell + ring of N adjacent cells. Subscription = join Socket.IO rooms per cell.
4. Postgres for orgs + worker profile + audit. Mongo for position history (time-series).
5. Adapter: Redis.
6. Hybrid because Postgres for org RBAC, Mongo time-series for breadcrumbs, Redis for hot proximity index.

database_design.indexes: workers (org_id). Breadcrumb time-series implicit.
database_design.queries: Update pos: pipeline = HGET old h3 → SREM old cell → SADD new cell → HSET new h3. Fan-out targets: SMEMBERS for current cell + adjacent.
database_design.transaction_strategy: None — Redis pipelines are atomic enough for this; if not, use MULTI/EXEC for old/new cell swap.

socket_io_design.events: Events: 'pos:update' (C→S 1Hz), 'pos:peer' (S→C peer position), 'visibility:subscribe' (C→S, sets H3 ring viewer cares about).

race_conditions_addressed: Cell transition: pipeline ensures SREM+SADD atomic enough; transient duplicate visibility (in both cells briefly) is acceptable.
Disconnect mid-update: TTL on cell membership + hash; stale entries cleared after 120s.
GPS jitter flap: accuracy filter rejects bad fixes; H3 resolution coarse (5km) reduces churn.
Org isolation: cell key prefix includes orgId; cross-org peer can't be in same Redis set.
scaling_considerations: 10x (500k workers): cell SETs become large for dense areas — increase H3 resolution for cities, reduce for rural; alternative shard by org. 100x: per-org dedicated Redis cluster.
observability: Metrics: pos_update_rate, cell_transition_count, roster_size_p99, subscribe_ms. Logs: org-scope violations. Logs: structured subscribe/emit/ack records with socketId, tenant/user id, room, sequence, permission decision, and reconnect token result. Traces: span from handler validation through persistence/outbox to Socket.IO emit and client ack. Alerts: ack_error_rate > 1%, reconnect_gap_count > 0.5% of sessions, fanout p99 > 500ms, or unauthorized_room_attempt_count > 0. Dashboard panels break down sock-031 by namespace, node, room size, and adapter lag.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A naive solution for Geo-presence with H3 spatial index for proximity-based fan-out handles the demo path but misses the presence tracking boundary: authorization, persistence, reconnect or retry behavior, rollback, and state drift incidents are not proved under production-shaped fixtures.

title: Whiteboard with live drawing strokes and infinite canvas
business_context: Miro-style whiteboard: live multi-user drawing on infinite canvas. Strokes stream as user draws (not just at stroke-end). Pan/zoom independent per user but cursor shared. Scale: 50k boards, 20 collaborators per hot board. If wrong: laggy drawing destroys creative flow.
constraints: Stroke points streamed at 30Hz during draw
failure_modes_to_handle: Eraser removes piece of another user's stroke
architecture_reasoning:
1. Stroke lifecycle: 'stroke:start' → 'stroke:point' (streaming) → 'stroke:end' (persists to Mongo). In-progress strokes live in Redis as ephemeral state.
2. Viewport culling: client tells server its viewport bbox; server returns only intersecting strokes.
3. Mongo for finalized strokes with geo-like index on bbox.
4. Adapter: Redis.

database_design.indexes: compound on (boardId, minX, maxX, minY, maxY) for viewport queries.
database_design.queries: Viewport fetch: db.strokes.find({boardId, maxX:{$gte:vp.minX}, minX:{$lte:vp.maxX}, maxY:{$gte:vp.minY}, minY:{$lte:vp.maxY}, deletedAt:null}).
database_design.transaction_strategy: Single-doc insert on stroke end; no tx.

socket_io_design.events: Events: 'board:open' (C→S {boardId, viewport}, ack with strokes), 'viewport:update' (C→S), 'stroke:start' (C→S {strokeId, color, width}), 'stroke:point' (C→S {strokeId, x, y, pressure}), 'stroke:end' (C→S {strokeId, points}), 'stroke:erase' (C→S), 'peer:stroke' (S→C).

race_conditions_addressed: Two simultaneous strokes: independent stroke IDs, no contention.
Abandoned stroke (disconnect): Redis TTL 60s cleans up live state.
Stroke during reconnect: stroke:end may not reach server; client retries on reconnect using cached points.
Erase concurrent with draw: erase sets deletedAt; viewport query filters out; new strokes unaffected.
scaling_considerations: 10x (100 collaborators): stroke point fan-out grows; throttle to 20Hz at scale. 100k strokes/board: viewport query + index handles; consider tile-based caching for hot boards.
observability: Metrics: stroke_persist_lag, in_progress_stroke_count, viewport_query_ms. Logs: structured subscribe/emit/ack records with socketId, tenant/user id, room, sequence, permission decision, and reconnect token result. Traces: span from handler validation through persistence/outbox to Socket.IO emit and client ack. Alerts: ack_error_rate > 1%, reconnect_gap_count > 0.5% of sessions, fanout p99 > 500ms, or unauthorized_room_attempt_count > 0. Dashboard panels break down sock-032 by namespace, node, room size, and adapter lag.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A naive solution for Whiteboard with live drawing strokes and infinite canvas handles the demo path but misses the live document annotations boundary: authorization, persistence, reconnect or retry behavior, rollback, and state drift incidents are not proved under production-shaped fixtures.

title: Mention digest with hourly batching and timezone-aware send window
business_context: Knowledge-base app sends 'mentioned 3 times today' digests instead of real-time noise. Users choose digest cadence (off, hourly, daily). Send only during user's awake hours. Scale: 2M users. If wrong: notification fatigue → unsubscribe.
constraints: Skip if user already saw mentions via web
Unsubscribe cascade — stop digest if user clicks 'too many'
Idempotency: never send same digest twice
architecture_reasoning:
1. Mentions tracked in PG with read_at column (set when user views in web). Digest worker scans every 30min for due users.
2. Per-user cadence + timezone + window in PG user_prefs.
3. Idempotency via digest_log table with (user_id, window_end) unique constraint.
4. Adapter: Redis (for web-read events triggering 'skip digest if seen').

database_design.indexes: mentions (mentioned_user_id, read_at, digested_at). digest_log UNIQUE.
database_design.queries: Find due users: SELECT user_id FROM digest_prefs WHERE cadence='hourly' AND ... time matches local hour mark. Pending mentions: SELECT count, ids FROM mentions WHERE mentioned_user_id=$1 AND digested_at IS NULL AND read_at IS NULL.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Digest send tx: INSERT digest_log (unique) → if OK then UPDATE mentions SET digested_at=now() → dispatch email. ON CONFLICT in digest_log = already sent, abort send.

race_conditions_addressed: DST transition: Luxon handles localized 'hour' arithmetic correctly.
User viewed before digest fires: mentions.read_at filter excludes them from digest.
Worker crash mid-batch: tx rollback for unfinished user; idempotency key prevents duplicate later.
Mid-window pref change: next tick uses new pref; current pending mentions still digest under prior rule.
scaling_considerations: 10x (20M users): chunk users across worker pool. Per-tz queue.
observability: Metrics: digest_sent_count, digest_skip_reason histogram, user_unsub_rate. Alerts: digest_skip > 50% (window logic bug). Logs: structured subscribe/emit/ack records with socketId, tenant/user id, room, sequence, permission decision, and reconnect token result. Traces: span from handler validation through persistence/outbox to Socket.IO emit and client ack. Alerts: ack_error_rate > 1%, reconnect_gap_count > 0.5% of sessions, fanout p99 > 500ms, or unauthorized_room_attempt_count > 0. Dashboard panels break down sock-033 by namespace, node, room size, and adapter lag.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A naive solution for Mention digest with hourly batching and timezone-aware send window handles the demo path but misses the notification streaming boundary: authorization, persistence, reconnect or retry behavior, rollback, and authorization denial count are not proved under production-shaped fixtures.

title: Vehicle fleet telemetry with geofence triggers and route playback
business_context: Fleet management: 5000 trucks reporting GPS + diagnostics at 1Hz. Geofence entry/exit triggers alerts (delivery confirmed, off-route). Operators can playback route at variable speed. Scale: 5k vehicles × 1Hz = 5k events/sec. If wrong: missed deliveries, fuel theft undetected.
failure_modes_to_handle: Cellular dead zone → backlog ingest on reconnect
Burst of 1000 events on group return
architecture_reasoning:
1. PG with PostGIS for geofences + telemetry. Geofence check via ST_Contains on insert.
2. Telemetry → TimescaleDB hypertable. Geofence breach via insert-trigger that runs spatial query and INSERTs alert.
3. Adapter: Redis. fleet:{customerId} room for fleet view; vehicle:{id} for single-vehicle drill.
4. Playback: HTTP API queries time range from hypertable.

database_design.indexes: GIST on geom for both tables. Telemetry composite for time-range scans.
database_design.queries: Ingest with breach check: INSERT telemetry + check by joining geofences WHERE ST_Contains(geofence.geom, point) → INSERT fence_event for transitions. Playback: SELECT * FROM telemetry WHERE vehicle_id=$1 AND ts BETWEEN $2 AND $3 ORDER BY ts.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Insert telemetry + compute breach + emit in single tx for atomicity.

race_conditions_addressed: Backlog on reconnect: ingester accepts batch with original ts; breach detection still correct via state diff.
Geofence redefined mid-occupancy: next telemetry recomputes; exit event fires if no longer inside new shape.
Burst of 1000 events: per-vehicle PRIMARY KEY (vehicle_id, ts) prevents duplicates from retry.
Multiple operators: socket.io room broadcast handles cleanly.
scaling_considerations: 10x (50k vehicles): hypertable compression after 24h. 100x: ingester partitioning by customer_id; PostGIS geom index size grows — periodic VACUUM critical.
observability: Metrics: ingest_to_emit_ms, breach_detect_count, playback_query_ms. Alerts: ingest backlog > 5s. Logs: structured subscribe/emit/ack records with socketId, tenant/user id, room, sequence, permission decision, and reconnect token result. Traces: span from handler validation through persistence/outbox to Socket.IO emit and client ack. Alerts: ack_error_rate > 1%, reconnect_gap_count > 0.5% of sessions, fanout p99 > 500ms, or unauthorized_room_attempt_count > 0. Dashboard panels break down sock-034 by namespace, node, room size, and adapter lag.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A naive solution for Vehicle fleet telemetry with geofence triggers and route playback handles the demo path but misses the iot sensor streaming boundary: authorization, persistence, reconnect or retry behavior, rollback, and query p95 latency are not proved under production-shaped fixtures.

title: Recording-of-record for compliance: deterministic SFU coordination + audit trail
business_context: Compliance-recorded WebRTC for healthcare consultations. Recording MUST start before any media flows; if recording fails, call must not proceed. Audit log captures consent, recording state, all participants. Scale: 1k concurrent calls. If wrong: HIPAA fine, lost license.
constraints: Recording start gated: no media before consent
Recording outage = call cannot proceed (no silent recording-off mode)
failure_modes_to_handle: Recording starts then SFU loses storage
Consent collected from N of M; M-th declines mid-call
Network partition between SFU and recorder
architecture_reasoning:
1. Call lifecycle: created (scheduled) → consenting (each participant must accept) → recording-ready → live (media flows) → ended.
2. Recording readiness: dedicated recorder service confirms write-handle open before participants get SFU transport.
3. Consent revocation: socket event marks participant 'left'; SFU pauses their tracks; recording continues for remaining.
4. Mongo for call state + audit log (hash-chained per sock-005).
5. Adapter: Redis. call:{id} room.

database_design.schema: const CallSchema = new Schema({ _id: String, sfuNodeId: String, recorderId: String, recordingPath: String, recordingState: { type: String, enum: ['none','starting','ready','live','ended','failed'], default: 'none' }, status: { type: String, enum: ['scheduled','consenting','recording-ready','live','ended'], default: 'scheduled' }, consents: { type: Map, of: { granted: Boolean, at: Date } }, createdAt: { type: Date, default: Date.now }, endedAt: Date });\nconst CallAuditSchema = new Schema({ callId: String, event: String, actorId: String, payload: Schema.Types.Mixed, prevHash: Buffer, rowHash: Buffer, at: { type: Date, default: Date.now } });\nCallAuditSchema.index({ callId: 1, at: 1 });
database_design.indexes: Call PK. Audit (callId, at).
database_design.queries: State transitions findOneAndUpdate with state guard.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Tx wrapping recorder.startRecording → if OK: call.recordingState='ready' → audit. If fail: state='failed' → audit + emit to call participants.

race_conditions_addressed: Two participants grant consent concurrently: findOneAndUpdate per participant atomic; final 'all consented' transition gated by recount inside that call's record.
Recording start race: state machine guard via findOneAndUpdate with status check.
Revoke after recording started: audit captures revoke; SFU pauses tracks; call continues for remaining consenters.
Recording failure mid-call: state→failed, all participants kicked, audit captures.
scaling_considerations: 10x: per-call cost stable; recorder pool autoscale.
observability: Metrics: consent_collect_p99, recording_start_failure_rate. Audit chain verification job. Alerts: recording_failure > 0.1% (storage issues). Logs: structured subscribe/emit/ack records with socketId, tenant/user id, room, sequence, permission decision, and reconnect token result. Traces: span from handler validation through persistence/outbox to Socket.IO emit and client ack. Alerts: ack_error_rate > 1%, reconnect_gap_count > 0.5% of sessions, fanout p99 > 500ms, or unauthorized_room_attempt_count > 0. Dashboard panels break down sock-035 by namespace, node, room size, and adapter lag.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A naive solution for Recording-of-record for compliance: deterministic SFU coordination + audit trail handles the demo path but misses the webrtc signaling boundary: authorization, persistence, reconnect or retry behavior, rollback, and cache stale hit rate are not proved under production-shaped fixtures.

title: Survey runtime with live response tally and abuse rate-limit
business_context: Live polls during conferences: thousands of attendees answer in real-time, presenter sees tally bars animate. Must resist abuse (one person voting 1000x). Scale: 50k voters per poll, peak 5k votes/sec at moment of question reveal. If wrong: contest result invalid, presenter embarrassment.
constraints: One vote per attendee per question
Anti-abuse: rate limit per IP + per device fingerprint
Presenter sees live; attendees see tally only after their vote
architecture_reasoning:
1. Votes: write to PG (durable, dedup via PK), increment Redis HASH for hot tally. Tally fan-out from Redis on a throttle (10Hz).
2. Anti-abuse: per-fingerprint single vote per question; per-IP rate limit; CAPTCHA challenge if suspicious.
3. Mongo for poll schema + audit traces (flexible payload).
4. Adapter: Redis.

database_design.indexes: votes PK ensures dedup. (poll_id, voted_at) for time-window query.
database_design.queries: INSERT vote ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING. Tally: GROUP BY option from PG (cold) or HGETALL from Redis (hot).
database_design.transaction_strategy: Vote: tx wrapping INSERT vote + Redis HINCRBY. If PG insert reports DO NOTHING (dup), skip Redis increment.

socket_io_design.events: Events: 'poll:open' (C→S {pollId}, ack with poll), 'vote' (C→S), 'tally' (S→C throttled), 'reveal' (S→C presenter command).

race_conditions_addressed: Same fp two tabs: PG PK rejects second.
Burst: PK + Redis incr provide atomicity; throttle holds fan-out.
Botnet: IP cap + fingerprint requirement; CAPTCHA on suspicious patterns.
Presenter disconnect: state in PG; reconnect picks up.
scaling_considerations: 10x (500k voters): PG vote table grows fast — partition by poll_id. Redis HINCRBY handles 100k/sec on single node.
observability: Metrics: vote_throughput, dup_vote_rate, rate_limit_hits, tally_fan_out_ms. Logs: structured subscribe/emit/ack records with socketId, tenant/user id, room, sequence, permission decision, and reconnect token result. Traces: span from handler validation through persistence/outbox to Socket.IO emit and client ack. Alerts: ack_error_rate > 1%, reconnect_gap_count > 0.5% of sessions, fanout p99 > 500ms, or unauthorized_room_attempt_count > 0. Dashboard panels break down sock-036 by namespace, node, room size, and adapter lag.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A naive solution for Survey runtime with live response tally and abuse rate-limit handles the demo path but misses the live form collaboration boundary: authorization, persistence, reconnect or retry behavior, rollback, and render commit time are not proved under production-shaped fixtures.

title: Saga orchestration: order → payment → inventory → shipping with compensations
business_context: E-commerce checkout: payment → reserve inventory → ship. Any step failure triggers compensating actions (refund payment, release inventory). Customer sees live progress + final status. Scale: 100k orders/day. If wrong: customer charged but no item shipped = chargeback storm.
constraints: Compensation reliable even if compensating step itself fails
Customer sees real progress not 'processing...' forever
failure_modes_to_handle: Payment OK, inventory fail → must refund
Compensation fails → escalate to ops, mark order as 'requires_intervention'
architecture_reasoning:
1. Saga state machine in PG. Each step has (pending, running, done, failed, compensating, compensated). BullMQ drives each step.
2. Idempotency via per-step (order_id, step) unique constraint on attempts.
3. Customer view: socket subscribes to order:{id}; events emitted on step transitions.
4. Compensation: orchestrator detects failure, enqueues comp jobs in reverse order.

database_design.indexes: saga_steps PK + (status) partial for sweepers.
database_design.queries: Step transition: UPDATE WITH guard. Compensation list: SELECT WHERE status='done' AND order_id ORDER BY step DESC.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Per-step tx: SELECT FOR UPDATE step row → execute → UPDATE status. Compensation enqueue in single tx with order status update.

race_conditions_addressed: Step worker crash: PG status='running' with attempts counter; sweeper resets stuck 'running' > N min back to 'pending'.
Compensation failure: bounded retries, escalation to intervention_required avoids infinite loops.
Concurrent subscribe ack vs live event: client merges step status from ack with subsequent events keyed by step name.
Idempotent step actions: each step writes external state with idempotency key (e.g., payment intent id). Retried run picks up existing key, no double charge.
scaling_considerations: 10x: per-worker concurrency + horizontal scaling. 100x: per-tenant queues.
observability: Metrics: step_duration_p99, compensation_count, intervention_required_count, attempts_to_success histogram. Alerts: intervention_required > 0 every 5min. Logs: structured subscribe/emit/ack records with socketId, tenant/user id, room, sequence, permission decision, and reconnect token result. Traces: span from handler validation through persistence/outbox to Socket.IO emit and client ack. Alerts: ack_error_rate > 1%, reconnect_gap_count > 0.5% of sessions, fanout p99 > 500ms, or unauthorized_room_attempt_count > 0. Dashboard panels break down sock-037 by namespace, node, room size, and adapter lag.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A naive solution for Saga orchestration: order → payment → inventory → shipping with compensations handles the demo path but misses the order job status boundary: authorization, persistence, reconnect or retry behavior, rollback, and heap retained bytes are not proved under production-shaped fixtures.

title: Inline code review with live thread updates and resolution sync
business_context: Code review (GitHub-PR-style). Reviewers comment on file lines; threads resolve. Other reviewers see live updates. Scale: 500 active PRs per org, 10 reviewers per hot PR. If wrong: review feels stale, sync issues.
constraints: Line anchor must follow code if rebased
failure_modes_to_handle: Comment on line N then rebase shifts N → N+5
Reply to comment in deleted thread
architecture_reasoning: 1. PG for comments + threads + resolution state. Line anchor stored as (commitSha, filePath, lineNumber, hunkContext) — server attempts to remap on rebase.\n2. LISTEN/NOTIFY for thread updates fan out.\n3. Adapter: Redis. pr:{id} room.

database_design.indexes: threads (pr_id, file_path, line) for file view fetch. comments (thread_id, id) for replies.
database_design.queries: Resolve: UPDATE WHERE NOT resolved.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Create thread + first comment in tx.

race_conditions_addressed: Two reviewers resolve at once: UPDATE WHERE NOT resolved makes second a no-op.
Reply to deleted thread: thread query returns no row; reply rejected.
Line shift on rebase: server recomputes line via hunk_context match in new commit; if not found, comment marked 'outdated' (not deleted).
Concurrent thread creation on same line: separate threads coexist; UI shows both.
scaling_considerations: 10x (5k PRs): per-PR room is cheap. Comments table grows — partition by created_at month.
observability: Metrics: thread_create_p99, resolved_thread_ratio, reply_count_per_thread histogram. Logs: structured subscribe/emit/ack records with socketId, tenant/user id, room, sequence, permission decision, and reconnect token result. Traces: span from handler validation through persistence/outbox to Socket.IO emit and client ack. Alerts: ack_error_rate > 1%, reconnect_gap_count > 0.5% of sessions, fanout p99 > 500ms, or unauthorized_room_attempt_count > 0. Dashboard panels break down sock-038 by namespace, node, room size, and adapter lag.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A naive solution for Inline code review with live thread updates and resolution sync handles the demo path but misses the collaborative editing boundary: authorization, persistence, reconnect or retry behavior, rollback, and render commit time are not proved under production-shaped fixtures.

title: Voice-message chat with playback state sync and per-segment reactions
business_context: Voice-first chat app. Voice messages stored as audio; users can react to specific timestamps. Playback state syncs across user's devices (paused-at-0:15 on phone, opens laptop). Scale: 500k DAU. If broken: voice continuity lost across devices.
constraints: Audio in object storage, metadata in DB
failure_modes_to_handle: Reaction timestamp drifts vs actual audio
architecture_reasoning: 1. Mongo for messages (flexible: voice/text). Postgres for playback state (per-user per-message simple state).\n2. Audio in S3, signed URL.\n3. Adapter: Redis. user:{id}, conv:{id}.

database_design.indexes: voice (convoId, createdAt). segReact (messageId). playback PK.
database_design.queries: Update position: INSERT ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE SET position_ms.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Persist domain event and outbox row in one transaction for Voice-message chat with playback state sync and per-segment reactions; emit Socket.IO updates only after commit.

socket_io_design.events: Events: 'conv:open', 'msg:voice' (S→C new voice msg), 'playback:position' (C→S), 'playback:synced' (S→C user's other devices), 'react:segment' (C→S), 'segment:reaction' (S→C).

race_conditions_addressed: Cross-device sync: server writes single record; OR-on-listened ensures latest-true wins.
Audio deleted: deletedAt filter excludes; client polls and removes.
Reaction drift: reactions store atMs, audio metadata immutable post-create.
scaling_considerations: 10x: playback writes bursty per scrub — debounce client to 1Hz.
observability: Metrics: playback_update_rate, segment_reaction_count. Logs: structured subscribe/emit/ack records with socketId, tenant/user id, room, sequence, permission decision, and reconnect token result. Traces: span from handler validation through persistence/outbox to Socket.IO emit and client ack. Alerts: ack_error_rate > 1%, reconnect_gap_count > 0.5% of sessions, fanout p99 > 500ms, or unauthorized_room_attempt_count > 0. Dashboard panels break down sock-039 by namespace, node, room size, and adapter lag.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A naive solution for Voice-message chat with playback state sync and per-segment reactions handles the demo path but misses the live chat boundary: authorization, persistence, reconnect or retry behavior, rollback, and state drift incidents are not proved under production-shaped fixtures.

title: Ops incident war-room with chronological event feed and ack receipt tracking
business_context: Incident response war-room: every event (alert fired, page sent, runbook triggered, mitigation applied) flows into a real-time feed. On-call must ack within 5min or escalate. Scale: 50 simul incidents, 20 responders per incident. If wrong: SLA breach, escalation chain breaks.
constraints: Escalation rules driven by ack timeouts
Chat-like commentary stream alongside system events
architecture_reasoning: 1. Events in PG append-only with monotonic id.\n2. LISTEN/NOTIFY broadcasts inserts to subscribed war-rooms.\n3. Escalation: BullMQ delayed job armed on alert fire, cancelled on first ack.

database_design.indexes: events (incident_id, id) for feed pagination. acks PK.
database_design.queries: Append event: INSERT + pg_notify. Ack: INSERT ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Insert event + pg_notify in trigger.

socket_io_design.events: Events: 'inc:open' (C→S ack with events + acks state), 'event:new' (S→C), 'comment' (C→S, server inserts as event_type='comment'), 'ack' (C→S), 'ack:received' (S→C).

race_conditions_addressed: Two acks: PK uniqueness; only first triggers escalation cancel.
Escalation vs ack race: BullMQ removeJob and ack record idempotent; even if escalation fires before remove, the escalation handler re-checks ack record before paging.
Network blip during ack: client retries; PG dedup.
Order preserved by PG SERIAL id.
scaling_considerations: 10x: per-incident listener pattern same as sock-002. 100x: shard incidents by id range across listener processes.
observability: Metrics: ack_latency_p99, escalation_fire_count, comment_rate. Logs: every escalation with incidentId, alertId, why. Logs: structured subscribe/emit/ack records with socketId, tenant/user id, room, sequence, permission decision, and reconnect token result. Traces: span from handler validation through persistence/outbox to Socket.IO emit and client ack. Alerts: ack_error_rate > 1%, reconnect_gap_count > 0.5% of sessions, fanout p99 > 500ms, or unauthorized_room_attempt_count > 0. Dashboard panels break down sock-040 by namespace, node, room size, and adapter lag.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A naive solution for Ops incident war-room with chronological event feed and ack receipt tracking handles the demo path but misses the real time dashboards boundary: authorization, persistence, reconnect or retry behavior, rollback, and cache stale hit rate are not proved under production-shaped fixtures.

title: Battle Royale lobby countdown with player join/leave during arming phase
business_context: 100-player BR lobby with countdown. Players join up to T-30s; latejoiners filled with bots. Countdown synced across all clients. Scale: 200 lobbies forming simultaneously. If wrong: countdown jitter ruins feel.
architecture_reasoning: 1. Lobby in Mongo with countdownStartedAt. Server tick interval (1Hz) emits remaining time = startedAt + 60s - now.\n2. Clients render local countdown using clientTimeOffset (computed via NTP-ish handshake) for smoothness.\n3. Adapter: Redis.

database_design.schema: const LobbySchema = new Schema({ _id: String, mode: String, capacity: { type: Number, default: 100 }, minPlayers: { type: Number, default: 60 }, players: [{ userId: String, joinedAt: Date, ready: Boolean }], status: { type: String, enum: ['waiting','countdown','starting','started','cancelled'], default: 'waiting' }, countdownStartedAt: Date, countdownSeconds: { type: Number, default: 60 }, gameServerId: String });
database_design.indexes: _id PK + (status, mode).
database_design.queries: Add player conditional: updateOne with $push and $size check; reject if full.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Single-doc updates atomic.

socket_io_design.events: Events: 'lobby:join' (C→S), 'lobby:leave', 'lobby:ready', 'tick' (S→C 1Hz), 'lobby:state' (S→C state changes), 'game:start' (S→C with server credentials).

race_conditions_addressed: Join+leave during countdown: atomic Mongo updates with size check.
Disconnect last second: tick handler reads fresh player count before transition.
Clock skew: server includes serverTs in tick; client uses for jitter compensation only — server is authoritative for transitions.
Restart mid-countdown: countdownStartedAt persisted; on restart ticker resumes from elapsed.
scaling_considerations: 10x: ticker becomes hot — partition by lobbyId hash across workers.
observability: Metrics: lobby_fill_p99, cancel_rate, start_latency. Alerts: cancel rate > 30% (matchmaking broken). Logs: structured subscribe/emit/ack records with socketId, tenant/user id, room, sequence, permission decision, and reconnect token result. Traces: span from handler validation through persistence/outbox to Socket.IO emit and client ack. Alerts: ack_error_rate > 1%, reconnect_gap_count > 0.5% of sessions, fanout p99 > 500ms, or unauthorized_room_attempt_count > 0. Dashboard panels break down sock-041 by namespace, node, room size, and adapter lag.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A naive solution for Battle Royale lobby countdown with player join/leave during arming phase handles the demo path but misses the multiplayer game state boundary: authorization, persistence, reconnect or retry behavior, rollback, and query p95 latency are not proved under production-shaped fixtures.

title: Buyer-seller chat tied to live order with negotiation timer
business_context: Marketplace negotiation: buyer initiates offer → seller has 30min to counter → 3 rounds max. Chat alongside. Scale: 50k active negotiations. If wrong: ambiguous deal state = disputes.
failure_modes_to_handle: Timer fires while user is typing
architecture_reasoning: 1. Negotiation state machine in PG. Offers + chat in same conversation table for unified timeline.\n2. Timers via BullMQ delayed jobs cancelled on turn change.\n3. Adapter: Redis.

database_design.schema: CREATE TABLE negotiations (id BIGSERIAL PK, item_id BIGINT, buyer_id BIGINT, seller_id BIGINT, status TEXT, current_round INT DEFAULT 0, current_turn TEXT, expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ, final_price_cents BIGINT, started_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now(), ended_at TIMESTAMPTZ);\nCREATE TABLE negotiation_events (id BIGSERIAL PK, neg_id BIGINT, actor_id BIGINT, type TEXT, payload JSONB, ts TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now());
database_design.indexes: events (neg_id, id).
database_design.queries: Counter: tx with current_turn check + round increment.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Per-action tx with status guard.

race_conditions_addressed: Out-of-turn submit: FOR UPDATE row lock + turn check.
Timer fires during transition: tx UPDATE WHERE status='active' guards.
Replay (same counter twice): client nonce + dedup on (negId, nonce); not shown but recommended.
Disconnect: state in DB; reconnect via subscribe.
scaling_considerations: 10x: per-neg lock isolates.
observability: Metrics: turn_latency, expired_rate, accept_rate. Logs: structured subscribe/emit/ack records with socketId, tenant/user id, room, sequence, permission decision, and reconnect token result. Traces: span from handler validation through persistence/outbox to Socket.IO emit and client ack. Alerts: ack_error_rate > 1%, reconnect_gap_count > 0.5% of sessions, fanout p99 > 500ms, or unauthorized_room_attempt_count > 0. Dashboard panels break down sock-042 by namespace, node, room size, and adapter lag.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A naive solution for Buyer-seller chat tied to live order with negotiation timer handles the demo path but misses the live auction boundary: authorization, persistence, reconnect or retry behavior, rollback, and query p95 latency are not proved under production-shaped fixtures.

title: Reading-in-channel granular presence for forum-style app
business_context: Reddit-style forum: see who's reading which thread right now ('30 viewing'). Per-thread accuracy without revealing identities (privacy). Scale: 1M threads, 100k concurrent readers. If wrong: thread count drifts.
constraints: Browser tab away (visibility hidden) drops out
failure_modes_to_handle: User opens 5 tabs of same thread
architecture_reasoning:
1. Per-thread Redis SET of socketIds. Add on subscribe, remove on disconnect/leave.
2. Throttled count broadcast: 5s aggregation per thread.
3. Mongo for thread metadata.
4. Adapter: Redis.

database_design.schema: // Mongo: threads collection (existing)\n// Redis: SET viewers:thread:{id} → socketIds, EX 600
database_design.indexes: N/A — Redis-driven hot path.
database_design.queries: Count: SCARD viewers:thread:{id}.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Persist domain event and outbox row in one transaction for Reading-in-channel granular presence for forum-style app; emit Socket.IO updates only after commit.

race_conditions_addressed: Multi-tab: each tab is a different socketId, both counted; this matches 'viewing intent' semantics.
Background tab: visibilitychange leaves room, decrements.
Reconnect spike: same user briefly counted twice (old socket TTL still in set); accept eventual consistency at 5s.
Viral spike: Redis SCARD O(1).
scaling_considerations: 10x: SET grows with viewers; SCARD remains O(1). 100x: shard viewer sets by threadId hash across Redis cluster.
observability: Metrics: active_threads, p99_count_emit_ms. Logs: structured subscribe/emit/ack records with socketId, tenant/user id, room, sequence, permission decision, and reconnect token result. Traces: span from handler validation through persistence/outbox to Socket.IO emit and client ack. Alerts: ack_error_rate > 1%, reconnect_gap_count > 0.5% of sessions, fanout p99 > 500ms, or unauthorized_room_attempt_count > 0. Dashboard panels break down sock-043 by namespace, node, room size, and adapter lag.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A naive solution for Reading-in-channel granular presence for forum-style app handles the demo path but misses the presence tracking boundary: authorization, persistence, reconnect or retry behavior, rollback, and cache stale hit rate are not proved under production-shaped fixtures.

title: Code line annotations with permalink and inline notification on resolution
business_context: Code review companion: add notes on any line of any file (not just PR). Notes live with the line, follow refactors via blame. Resolution notifies original author. Scale: 100k repos × 50 active notes each.
constraints: Note anchored to (repo, file, sha, line)
Follow line through commits via blame
architecture_reasoning: 1. PG for notes. blame lookup via library (libgit2 binding) on note display.\n2. Adapter: Redis. file:{repo}:{path} room while viewing.

database_design.schema: CREATE TABLE code_notes (id BIGSERIAL PK, repo TEXT, file_path TEXT, anchor_sha TEXT, line INT, author_id BIGINT, body TEXT, resolved_at TIMESTAMPTZ, resolved_by BIGINT, current_path TEXT, current_line INT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now());\nCREATE INDEX ON code_notes (repo, current_path, current_line) WHERE resolved_at IS NULL;
database_design.indexes: (repo, current_path, current_line) partial for active notes view.
database_design.queries: View file notes: SELECT WHERE current_path=$1 AND repo=$2.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Refactor remap job: tx update current_path/current_line in bulk.

race_conditions_addressed: Two resolutions: WHERE resolved_at IS NULL makes second a no-op.
Renamed file: refactorRemap updates current_path; subscribers in old file room don't see; new file room subscribers see remapped.
Author left org: notification routes per current notification preferences for that user (may queue indefinitely).
scaling_considerations: 10x: blame lookup expensive — batch in refactor worker, not on view.
observability: Metrics: remap_success_rate, note_resolve_rate. Logs: structured subscribe/emit/ack records with socketId, tenant/user id, room, sequence, permission decision, and reconnect token result. Traces: span from handler validation through persistence/outbox to Socket.IO emit and client ack. Alerts: ack_error_rate > 1%, reconnect_gap_count > 0.5% of sessions, fanout p99 > 500ms, or unauthorized_room_attempt_count > 0. Dashboard panels break down sock-044 by namespace, node, room size, and adapter lag.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A naive solution for Code line annotations with permalink and inline notification on resolution handles the demo path but misses the live document annotations boundary: authorization, persistence, reconnect or retry behavior, rollback, and cache stale hit rate are not proved under production-shaped fixtures.

title: Push notification with FCM/APNs delivery and click-through tracking
business_context: Mobile app: send push notification, track if user tapped, deep-link to in-app screen. Click-through ratio measured. Scale: 5M devices, 1M pushes/day. If wrong: ad team can't measure attribution.
constraints: Click-through reported by app on tap
architecture_reasoning:
1. Devices in PG with FCM/APNs token + last_seen_at. Pushes through provider SDK; receipts ingested.
2. Tap events come via socket from app when online, batched.
3. Mongo for push events history (high write rate).
4. Adapter: Redis.

database_design.indexes: devices UNIQUE per user+token. PushEvent (userId, dispatchedAt).
database_design.queries: Click report: updateOne({_id: pushId, openedAt: null}, {$set: {openedAt: now}}).
database_design.transaction_strategy: None — single doc updates.

race_conditions_addressed: Token expired: error code triggers token nullification; next rotate replaces.
Multiple devices: per-device PushEvent; aggregate per user for opens.
Tap offline: app caches pushId tap and replays on next connect.
Provider outage: errorCode recorded; retry job handles.
scaling_considerations: 10x: PushEvent collection large — TTL or archive.
observability: Metrics: dispatch_success_rate, delivery_rate, open_rate, p99 dispatch_time. Logs: structured subscribe/emit/ack records with socketId, tenant/user id, room, sequence, permission decision, and reconnect token result. Traces: span from handler validation through persistence/outbox to Socket.IO emit and client ack. Alerts: ack_error_rate > 1%, reconnect_gap_count > 0.5% of sessions, fanout p99 > 500ms, or unauthorized_room_attempt_count > 0. Dashboard panels break down sock-045 by namespace, node, room size, and adapter lag.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A naive solution for Push notification with FCM/APNs delivery and click-through tracking handles the demo path but misses the notification streaming boundary: authorization, persistence, reconnect or retry behavior, rollback, and rollback elapsed minutes are not proved under production-shaped fixtures.

title: Smart-home hub with device control fan-out and offline command queue
business_context: Smart-home: 50 devices per home, user app controls them. Devices report state; user app shows live state and issues commands. Offline device commands queue locally on hub and replay on reconnect. Scale: 1M homes.
failure_modes_to_handle: Device sends stale state after long offline
architecture_reasoning:
1. Hubs in home connect to nearest edge Socket.IO node. Devices connect through hub.
2. State in Mongo per device.
3. Commands: PUT into device queue (PG) when offline; deliver on connect; ack triggers removal.
4. Adapter: Redis.

database_design.indexes: devices (homeId). commands (device_id, delivered_at).
database_design.queries: Pending: SELECT WHERE device_id=$1 AND acked_at IS NULL ORDER BY id.
database_design.transaction_strategy: None — single-op.

socket_io_design.rooms: home:{id} for app users, device:{id} for device direct.
socket_io_design.middleware_order: auth (cert for devices, JWT for app)

race_conditions_addressed: Duplicate command from retry: idempotencyKey UNIQUE.
Device offline at command time: queued; flushed on next announce.
Stale state after long offline: server takes latest; device clock not trusted, server time used.
User reassignment: socket room left + rejoined.
scaling_considerations: 10x (10M homes): edge routing by region; per-region Redis adapter.
observability: Metrics: cmd_dispatch_latency, queue_depth_per_device, online_device_ratio. Logs: structured subscribe/emit/ack records with socketId, tenant/user id, room, sequence, permission decision, and reconnect token result. Traces: span from handler validation through persistence/outbox to Socket.IO emit and client ack. Alerts: ack_error_rate > 1%, reconnect_gap_count > 0.5% of sessions, fanout p99 > 500ms, or unauthorized_room_attempt_count > 0. Dashboard panels break down sock-046 by namespace, node, room size, and adapter lag.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A naive solution for Smart-home hub with device control fan-out and offline command queue handles the demo path but misses the iot sensor streaming boundary: authorization, persistence, reconnect or retry behavior, rollback, and state drift incidents are not proved under production-shaped fixtures.

title: Live captions broadcast alongside WebRTC media
business_context: Accessible video meeting: server-side speech-to-text generates captions; broadcasts as overlay to all participants. Scale: 1k concurrent meetings. If wrong: deaf/HoH users excluded.
architecture_reasoning: 1. SFU pipes audio to STT (Whisper API or Google). STT emits interim + final. Server tags by speaker via SFU mapping.\n2. PG for caption transcript.\n3. Socket fan-out captions.

database_design.schema: CREATE TABLE captions (id BIGSERIAL PK, call_id BIGINT, speaker_id BIGINT, lang TEXT, text TEXT, is_final BOOLEAN, ts TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now());\nCREATE INDEX ON captions (call_id, ts);
database_design.indexes: (call_id, ts).
database_design.queries: Append final caption.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Persist domain event and outbox row in one transaction for Live captions broadcast alongside WebRTC media; emit Socket.IO updates only after commit.

race_conditions_addressed: Interim → final transition: client clears interim on final.
Speaker change mid-utterance: STT engine handles or splits; server tags accordingly.
STT failure: caption stream pauses; alert sent.
scaling_considerations: 10x: per-call STT cost is dominant; pool STT workers; multiplex audio to single STT process per call.
observability: Metrics: stt_latency_p99, caption_drop_rate. Logs: structured subscribe/emit/ack records with socketId, tenant/user id, room, sequence, permission decision, and reconnect token result. Traces: span from handler validation through persistence/outbox to Socket.IO emit and client ack. Alerts: ack_error_rate > 1%, reconnect_gap_count > 0.5% of sessions, fanout p99 > 500ms, or unauthorized_room_attempt_count > 0. Dashboard panels break down sock-047 by namespace, node, room size, and adapter lag.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A naive solution for Live captions broadcast alongside WebRTC media handles the demo path but misses the webrtc signaling boundary: authorization, persistence, reconnect or retry behavior, rollback, and query p95 latency are not proved under production-shaped fixtures.

title: Collaborative diagramming with shape lock and conflict-free move
business_context: Lucidchart-style: drag/drop shapes with peers. Shape-level lock; CRDT-like move (no lock for position updates, but creator wins on conflicting topology changes). Scale: 50k diagrams.
constraints: Shape lock for edit (text/style); move is lock-free
failure_modes_to_handle: Two users move same shape simultaneously
architecture_reasoning: 1. Mongo for diagram + shapes (subdocs or separate collection).\n2. Move events not persisted per-tick; persisted on move-end. Live broadcast is throttled.\n3. Shape-level lock via Redis SETNX for non-move edits.

database_design.schema: const DiagramSchema = new Schema({ _id: String, name: String, version: { type: Number, default: 0 } });\nconst ShapeSchema = new Schema({ _id: String, diagramId: { type: String, index: true }, type: String, x: Number, y: Number, w: Number, h: Number, z: Number, text: String, style: Schema.Types.Mixed, version: { type: Number, default: 1 }, deletedAt: Date });\nconst EdgeSchema = new Schema({ _id: String, diagramId: String, fromShapeId: String, toShapeId: String, style: Schema.Types.Mixed });
database_design.indexes: shapes (diagramId, deletedAt). edges (diagramId).
database_design.queries: Move-end: updateOne with conditional set if version match. Topology change: tx of shape delete + edge cleanup.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Topology tx (e.g., delete shape + dangling edges).

race_conditions_addressed: Two users move same shape: last save wins via version check; live broadcast shows last move during drag (visual flicker, acceptable).
Topology tx: shape delete + edge cleanup atomic.
Lock + edit version conflict: lock ensures no concurrent edits; version check catches stale.
Diagram with 10k shapes: pagination via viewport-style queries; not shown but recommended.
scaling_considerations: 10x: 10k shapes per diagram fine in Mongo; move live broadcast at 60Hz × 50 collaborators × 1k diagrams = bus pressure — throttle move to 30Hz at high collaborator counts.
observability: Metrics: move_broadcast_rate, lock_conflict_rate, topology_tx_p99. Logs: structured subscribe/emit/ack records with socketId, tenant/user id, room, sequence, permission decision, and reconnect token result. Traces: span from handler validation through persistence/outbox to Socket.IO emit and client ack. Alerts: ack_error_rate > 1%, reconnect_gap_count > 0.5% of sessions, fanout p99 > 500ms, or unauthorized_room_attempt_count > 0. Dashboard panels break down sock-048 by namespace, node, room size, and adapter lag.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A naive solution for Collaborative diagramming with shape lock and conflict-free move handles the demo path but misses the live form collaboration boundary: authorization, persistence, reconnect or retry behavior, rollback, and heap retained bytes are not proved under production-shaped fixtures.

title: Bulk import progress with per-row error reporting and retry
business_context: Data import: user uploads 100k-row CSV. Each row validated + inserted. UI shows progress bar + skipped-row table. User can retry skipped rows after fixing CSV. Scale: 500 simul imports.
architecture_reasoning: 1. PG for import job state + per-row error table. Mongo for the actual imported records (varying schemas across import types).\n2. Worker processes CSV in batches; emits progress.\n3. Resume via last_processed_row.

database_design.schema: CREATE TABLE imports (id BIGSERIAL PK, user_id BIGINT, file_hash TEXT, total_rows INT, processed_rows INT DEFAULT 0, succeeded INT DEFAULT 0, failed INT DEFAULT 0, status TEXT, last_processed_row INT DEFAULT 0, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now(), UNIQUE (user_id, file_hash));\nCREATE TABLE import_errors (id BIGSERIAL PK, import_id BIGINT, row INT, error TEXT, raw JSONB);
database_design.indexes: imports UNIQUE for dedup. errors (import_id, row).
database_design.queries: Update progress: UPDATE imports SET ... WHERE id.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Per-batch tx: insert succeeded rows + update last_processed_row + insert errors.

socket_io_design.events: Events: 'import:subscribe', 'import:cancel', 'progress' (S→C every 1s or batch boundary), 'error:row' (S→C immediate for newly-failed rows).

race_conditions_addressed: File reupload: UNIQUE (user_id, file_hash) returns existing import.
Crash mid-import: last_processed_row persisted in batches; resume from that row on next worker pickup.
Cancel mid-batch: per-row check of Redis cancel flag inside loop; flushes partial batch.
Subscribe race: ack returns current state then live events fill in.
scaling_considerations: 10x: parallel workers per import (split CSV by row range).
observability: Metrics: import_throughput_rows_per_sec, error_rate per import, p99 batch_flush_ms. Logs: structured subscribe/emit/ack records with socketId, tenant/user id, room, sequence, permission decision, and reconnect token result. Traces: span from handler validation through persistence/outbox to Socket.IO emit and client ack. Alerts: ack_error_rate > 1%, reconnect_gap_count > 0.5% of sessions, fanout p99 > 500ms, or unauthorized_room_attempt_count > 0. Dashboard panels break down sock-049 by namespace, node, room size, and adapter lag.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A naive solution for Bulk import progress with per-row error reporting and retry handles the demo path but misses the order job status boundary: authorization, persistence, reconnect or retry behavior, rollback, and idempotency replay count are not proved under production-shaped fixtures.

title: Capstone: multi-doc workspace with cross-doc references and link-preview updates
business_context: Notion/Roam-style workspace: docs link to other docs via @-references. When linked doc's title changes, all links update live. Backlinks panel shows where doc is referenced. Scale: 1M docs, 10M references, 500 concurrent editors. If wrong: stale links everywhere, knowledge graph rots.
constraints: Title update propagates to all link previews live
Permission-aware (don't leak title to viewers without access)
failure_modes_to_handle: Mass-rename triggers thousands of link updates
Deleted doc — links show as 'untitled' or broken
architecture_reasoning:
1. Docs in Mongo (content). References in Postgres (relational, perfect for graph queries with joins/CTEs).
2. Title update → tx updates Mongo doc → tx inserts into references_change_log → worker fans out to all docs referencing it via Socket.IO rooms.
3. Backlinks computed on-demand via PG join; cached in Redis.
4. Permission filter on emit: server resolves viewer access before sending title update.

database_design.indexes: doc_refs PK for upsert dedup, (dst_doc_id) for backlinks query.
database_design.queries: Backlinks: SELECT src_doc_id FROM doc_refs WHERE dst_doc_id=$1. Forward refs: SELECT dst_doc_id FROM doc_refs WHERE src_doc_id=$1.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Title update: Mongo session tx wrapping doc title set. After commit: PG INSERT into title_change_log triggering worker.

race_conditions_addressed: Mass rename: per-referencing-doc fetch + permission check is O(N); use server-side rate-limit and batched broadcast to avoid event-loop starvation.
Cross-tenant link attempt: workspace_id stored on each ref; emit filter uses workspaceId — even if attacker links, cross-tenant viewer never gets title update.
Deleted doc: title query returns null; UI shows 'untitled'.
Cycle: PG schema allows it (no FK to dst); rendering library is responsible for cycle detection on display.
Concurrent ref-set update: DELETE+INSERT in tx — second writer's tx sees first's commit and overwrites consistently (last save wins for refs).
scaling_considerations: 10x (10M docs): per-title-change fan-out scales with ref count; popular docs (workspace home page) have 10k+ refs — switch to push-via-Redis-pub/sub-keyed-by-dstDocId so referencing docs subscribe on open. 100x: introduce graph DB (DGraph) for backlink queries; PG remains for transactional refs.
observability: Metrics: title_propagate_p99 (max time to reach all referencing docs), backlinks_query_ms, cross_tenant_emit_block_count. Logs: every title change with old/new + actor. Alerts: title_propagate p99 > 5s (fan-out lag). Logs: structured subscribe/emit/ack records with socketId, tenant/user id, room, sequence, permission decision, and reconnect token result. Traces: span from handler validation through persistence/outbox to Socket.IO emit and client ack. Alerts: ack_error_rate > 1%, reconnect_gap_count > 0.5% of sessions, fanout p99 > 500ms, or unauthorized_room_attempt_count > 0. Dashboard panels break down sock-050 by namespace, node, room size, and adapter lag.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A naive solution for Capstone: multi-doc workspace with cross-doc references and link-preview updates handles the demo path but misses the collaborative editing boundary: authorization, persistence, reconnect or retry behavior, rollback, and heap retained bytes are not proved under production-shaped fixtures.
